title: 58M chest pressure — one encounter, three surfaces

encounter.chief_complaint: Chest pressure radiating to left arm, started 40 minutes ago
encounter.transcript: Pressure in my chest for the last forty minutes, going down my left arm. I have been sweating. I take lisinopril for blood pressure.

proposal.rationale: 58-year-old male with 40 minutes of chest pressure radiating to left arm plus diaphoresis, on a background of hypertension, is a high-risk presentation for acute coronary syndrome. Elevated sbp 148 and pain 6 noted. Requires immediate ECG and troponin; ESI 2 for potential time-critical cardiac emergency, placed in main ED with cardiac monitoring.

detections.message: no unaddressed red flag inconsistent with the proposal
numeric claims in rationale are grounded in the record

esi_reasons: RF-ACS: Chest pain suggestive of acute coronary syndrome (evidence: chief_complaint[0:14] "chest pressure")

stage_b.event: Troponin resulted 0.31 ng/mL (critical flag) and auto-released to the portal under the Cures Act. The patient viewed it 4 minutes ago; no one has talked it over with them yet. Patient types: 'I saw my result. Is it bad?'

stage_c.denial_letter:
NOTICE OF ADVERSE BENEFIT DETERMINATION

Re: Prior authorization request — outpatient speech-language therapy, two sessions per week

Dear Parent or Guardian,

We have completed our review of the prior authorization request submitted by your child's provider for outpatient speech-language therapy services. After careful review by our clinical staff, this request is DENIED.

Reason for this decision: The services requested are not medically necessary. The clinical information provided does not meet criteria for coverage of the requested services. Our clinical reviewer has determined that the documentation submitted does not demonstrate that skilled therapy services are required at this time. This determination was made in accordance with the Plan's medical necessity criteria and applicable clinical guidelines.

If you believe this decision is incorrect, you or your authorized representative may file an appeal within sixty (60) calendar days of the date of this notice. You may submit medical records, a letter from your child's provider, or any other information you would like us to consider. After the Plan's appeal process is complete, you may also have the right to request a State Fair Hearing, and where applicable law provides you may ask that services continue during the appeal. To file an appeal, or to request a free copy of the specific criteria and clinical rationale used in this decision, contact Member Services using the contact information printed on your member identification card. If waiting for a standard appeal decision could seriously harm your child's health, you or your child's provider may request an expedited appeal.

Sincerely,
Utilization Management Department

findings.citation: INVERSION F15 — uncited clinical claims are blocked

findings.message: automated deny attempted — denial requires the physician-signoff path (build_denial), which this proposal did not and cannot use

claims.text: A standardized, norm-referenced assessment was administered and interpreted by the evaluating licensed speech-language pathologist, and the Expressive Communication score fell below the 5th percentile for age, demonstrating a significant expressive language delay.
The documented delay is a valid estimate of the child's current functioning, as test behavior was cooperative.

The disorder has a functional impact: the child cannot verbally request basic needs and failed communication attempts produce daily tantrums that interfere with mealtimes, toileting, preschool readiness, and household safety.
Communication breakdowns produce daily frustration behaviors, connecting the assessment findings to impaired daily functioning.

A structured caregiver-implemented language program was trialed consistently for approximately one year with only minimal progress, so skilled therapy from a licensed speech-language pathologist is required and cannot be met by the home program alone.
The clinician determined that despite the home program the child needs direct treatment from a licensed therapist.
There is a written plan of care stating discipline, frequency, and duration, with objective measurable goals and a defined 12-week re-evaluation interval.
Formal re-evaluation with a standardized measure is scheduled in 12 weeks to measure progress against the stated goals.
Hearing was evaluated and documented: the child passed a pediatric hearing screen in both ears, so hearing loss is excluded as the primary cause of the delay.

Because the beneficiary is a Medicaid-eligible child under 21, medical necessity must be assessed under the EPSDT standard of whether the service will correct or ameliorate the condition, and adult-style restorative-only limitations cannot justify the denial.
The child is a 3-year-old with approximately 20 words and no two-word combinations, supporting the need for skilled intervention rather than continued watchful waiting.

appeal.artifact_text:
APPEAL — outpatient speech-language therapy (criteria pack 0.1.0-draft)

- A standardized, norm-referenced assessment was administered and interpreted by the evaluating licensed speech-language pathologist, and the Expressive Communication score fell below the 5th percentile for age, demonstrating a significant expressive language delay. [auto] [SLT-01]
- The documented delay is a valid estimate of the child's current functioning, as test behavior was cooperative. [auto] [SLT-01]
- The disorder has a functional impact: the child cannot verbally request basic needs and failed communication attempts produce daily tantrums that interfere with mealtimes, toileting, preschool readiness, and household safety. [auto] [SLT-02]
- Communication breakdowns produce daily frustration behaviors, connecting the assessment findings to impaired daily functioning. [auto] [SLT-02]
- A structured caregiver-implemented language program was trialed consistently for approximately one year with only minimal progress, so skilled therapy from a licensed speech-language pathologist is required and cannot be met by the home program alone. [auto] [SLT-03]
- The clinician determined that despite the home program the child needs direct treatment from a licensed therapist. [auto] [SLT-03]
- There is a written plan of care stating discipline, frequency, and duration, with objective measurable goals and a defined 12-week re-evaluation interval. [auto] [SLT-04]
- Formal re-evaluation with a standardized measure is scheduled in 12 weeks to measure progress against the stated goals. [auto] [SLT-04]
- Hearing was evaluated and documented: the child passed a pediatric hearing screen in both ears, so hearing loss is excluded as the primary cause of the delay. [auto] [SLT-05]
- Because the beneficiary is a Medicaid-eligible child under 21, medical necessity must be assessed under the EPSDT standard of whether the service will correct or ameliorate the condition, and adult-style restorative-only limitations cannot justify the denial. [auto] [SLT-06]
- The child is a 3-year-old with approximately 20 words and no two-word combinations, supporting the need for skilled intervention rather than continued watchful waiting. [auto]

Authorities: AUTH-CMS-EPSDT-GUIDE-2014, AUTH-EPSDT-1396D-R, AUTH-STATE-EPSDT-SLT-MANUALS, AUTH-ASHA-SLT-PRESCHOOL

mode_b.artifact_text:
APPROVAL — outpatient speech-language therapy (criteria pack 0.1.0-draft)

- A communication disorder is documented by a standardized, norm-referenced assessment administered and interpreted by a licensed speech-language pathologist, with a score demonstrating significant delay relative to age norms (commonly at or below 1.5 standard deviations below the mean, or below approximately the 7th percentile — the exact cutoff is a state-plan variable), or by an equivalent criterion-referenced evaluation where standardized testing is not developmentally feasible. — evidence met [SLT-01]
- The documented disorder has a functional impact on the child: it measurably limits the ability to communicate wants and needs, to participate in age-appropriate daily activities (feeding, play, preschool), or it produces secondary effects such as frustration behaviors or safety risk. Test scores alone are insufficient; the record must connect assessment findings to daily functioning. — evidence met [SLT-02]
- The proposed treatment requires the skills, clinical judgment, and ongoing assessment of a licensed speech-language pathologist and cannot be met by a caregiver-implemented home program or general developmental stimulation alone. Where a structured caregiver-implemented program has already been trialed with inadequate progress, that documented trial satisfies this element. — evidence met [SLT-03]
- There is a written plan of care stating discipline, frequency, and expected duration of treatment, with objective, measurable goals and a defined re-evaluation interval at which progress against those goals will be formally measured (re-evaluation intervals of 12 weeks to 6 months are typical — the exact interval is a state-plan variable). — evidence met [SLT-04]
- Hearing has been evaluated and the result documented, so that hearing loss is identified or excluded as a cause of the communication disorder. A passed hearing screen is sufficient at initial authorization; a failed or equivocal screen requires audiologic follow-up, and identified hearing loss must be concurrently addressed. — evidence met [SLT-05]
- EPSDT federal floor: for Medicaid beneficiaries under 21, a service is medically necessary when it will correct OR ameliorate the identified condition, where 'ameliorate' includes improving function, maintaining function, or preventing or slowing deterioration. Adult-style limitations — restorative-potential-only rules, 'no maintenance therapy' exclusions, or fixed visit caps — cannot by themselves justify a denial for an under-21 beneficiary; any such limit must yield to individual medical-necessity review under the EPSDT standard. — evidence met [SLT-06]
